# Software Developer Test Project 2026

## Project Goals

- **Spend a few hours:** Put your best foot forward, submit a project that shows your best work. The evaluation will be code reviews and discussions of your implementation and architecture choices.

- **Download the empty project:** [Here](#). We have started you with an index.html file and an empty controller. When you are done you can zip it up and share it with the team for review either as a download link or zip attachment to an email. If you share the project by email keep in mind the zip has exe files inside it and Gmail and other virus scanners may block the email. To avoid issues, rename the extension to something like `MyProject.CHANGEME`

- **End-to-End Flow that Works:** Implement both server side and client side logic in a primitive end to end flow that runs without errors for a few small features. The key goal is to show a working end to end proof of concept with enough C# and JavaScript code that we can get a feel for your coding style and have some interesting classes to discuss in the code review.

  - **Simple is better:** The less boilerplate junk the better - if you can pick a barebones project type or delete all the default controllers and files you are not using it will help us focus on your code

  - **Focus on Code:** Spend most of your time actually writing code so that we can have a substantial work product to review. For the purpose of this exercise we really need to see original code and are not interested in seeing a lot of framework or template usage.

## File & Directory Browsing Single Page App

The task is to create a web service API that allows users to query the contents of a directory on the web server and a single page web app that can be used to search and browse folders and files. A server side home directory should be configurable via variable.

Client side, the application should be deep-linkable (the state of the UI should be kept in the URL). All of the UI work should be done client side via JavaScript that renders HTML - do not render HTML server side. Styling is not as important for this task, we are more concerned with functionality. Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library).

The goal is to produce a functional proof-of-concept to let us see how you think. It is important to provide a decent body of code, so that we can talk about your logic and choices. It isn't important to make the UI "pretty".

The point of this exercise is to end up with a body of work that is big enough that our team can review it and evaluate your design and coding style and skills.

## Requirements

- Ensure your solution builds in Visual Studio (any version 2022 or newer is acceptable), Rider, VS Code, or command line SDK tools
- Web API that allows Browse and Search Files & Folders and returns JSON
- Deep linkable URL pattern
- SPA (Single Page App using JavaScript)
- Upload/download files from the browser
- Show file and folder counts and sizes for the current view
- Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library)

## Bonus

- Entire component contained in a dialog widget, with a trigger element (button, etc)
- Delete, move, copy files and folders
- Highly performant

content.thinking:
Good. So the ZIP matches exactly what we have extracted. Now I have a complete picture. Let me think through all the findings.

## Analysis

### 1. Project Structure and Build Configuration
The project is a standard ASP.NET Core Web API project targeting `net8.0`. It needs to be retargeted to `net10.0`.

**csproj key points:**
- Uses `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web` (correct for a web API)
- `TargetFramework` = `net8.0` → needs to become `net10.0`
- `Nullable` = `enable` (good for modern C#)
- `ImplicitUsings` = `enable` (good)
- No NuGet package references (besides the implicit ASP.NET Core ones)
- No `<PackageReference>` elements - this is a good thing for a minimal project
- No `<OutputType>` specified (implicitly `Exe` from `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web`)
- Includes `wwwroot\index.html` as a `None` item

**sln key points:**
- Standard Visual Studio 2022 solution file format
- Single project `{A08F391E-2070-4DE8-87D9-CED3E0A05AE9}` = `TestProject.csproj`
- Debug|Any CPU and Release|Any CPU configurations
- Solution GUID is `{8F48BCB7-F418-4A32-9C88-6124323FDA8D}`
- The GUID `{FAE04EC0-301F-11D3-BF4B-00C04F79EFBC}` is the standard C# project type GUID (should stay the same)
- VisualStudioVersion = 17.7.34003.232 (VS 2022 17.7) - this is fine for dotnet 10 as well

### 2. Program.cs - Middleware Pipeline
Current pipeline:
```csharp
builder.Services.AddControllers();          // Registers controller services
app.UseHttpsRedirection();                  // Redirects HTTP to HTTPS
app.UseStaticFiles();                       // Serves static files from wwwroot
app.MapControllers();                       // Maps attribute-routed controllers
app.Run();                                  // Runs the app
```

This is a very minimal setup. The task requires:
- Web API that allows Browse and Search Files & Folders and returns JSON
- Upload/download files from the browser
- Bonus: Delete, move, copy files and folders
- A configurable server-side home directory (via variable)
- SPA with deep-linkable URLs

What needs to change:
- The pipeline is too minimal. We'll need proper middleware ordering.
- Static files should serve the SPA (index.html) from wwwroot
- Need CORS? Not mentioned, but potentially useful for development.
- `UseHttpsRedirection()` may or may not be desired - for development it can be kept, but could be conditionally applied.
- For a SPA, we typically want `UseDefaultFiles()` before `UseStaticFiles()` so `index.html` is served at root. Currently `launchUrl` is `index.html`, which works but `UseDefaultFiles` would be cleaner.

### 3. Controllers and Routing
- `TestController` uses `[ApiController]` attribute and `[Route("[controller]")]` convention
- The `[controller]` token in route templates resolves to `Test` (the controller name without "Controller" suffix)
- It has a single `[HttpGet]` action returning a plain string
- Implicit `[ApiController]` adds model validation, problem details for 400 responses, etc.
- The controller uses `ILogger<TestController>` via DI

What needs to change:
- A new controller (e.g., `FilesController`) needs to be added for the file operations
- The `TestController` could be removed or kept as a health check
- The new controller needs endpoints for:
  - Browse (GET list of files/folders in a path)
  - Search (GET search files/folders by name)
  - Upload (POST file upload)
  - Download (GET file download)
  - Bonus: DELETE, MOVE (PUT/PATCH?), COPY (POST?)
- Must return JSON for Browse and Search
- Need to use `[Route("api/[controller]")]` or specific route templates for the API

### 4. Configuration for Home Directory
The task says: "A server side home directory should be configurable via variable."

Current `appsettings.json` has only:
```json
{
  "Logging": { ... },
  "AllowedHosts": "*"
}
```

Options for the configurable home directory:
- `appsettings.json` / `appsettings.Development.json`: Add a `"HomeDirectory"` key
- Environment variable: `HOME_DIRECTORY` or `FileService:HomeDirectory`
- Command-line argument
- User secrets (for development)

Most flexible: Use the Options pattern with `IOptions<T>` (or `IOptionsMonitor<T>`). Define a `FileServiceOptions` class with a `HomeDirectory` property, and bind it from configuration (which can come from appsettings, env vars, CLI args, etc.).

The .gitignore already ignores `appsettings.*.local.json` and `secrets.json`, so local overrides are already handled.

### 5. Static Files and SPA Hosting
- `app.UseStaticFiles()` serves files from `wwwroot/`
- The `.gitignore` has patterns for `wwwroot/dist/`, `wwwroot/lib/`
- The `launchSettings.json` sets `launchUrl: "index.html"` and `launchBrowser: true`
- For proper SPA deep-linking, we might need `app.UseDefaultFiles()` and `app.UseStaticFiles()` in the right order, and a fallback to `index.html` for client-side routing

For deep-linkable SPA URLs, we'd need to handle SPAs by serving `index.html` for any route not matching an API endpoint or static file. This can be done with a simple middleware or `app.MapFallbackToFile("index.html")` (available in .NET 8+).

### 6. Key Observations for dotnet 10 Upgrade
- Changing `net8.0` to `net10.0` in the csproj is the primary change
- The `VisualStudioVersion` in the .sln (17.7) is fine for .NET 10 (VS 2022 works with .NET 10 SDK)
- No other framework-specific changes needed since the OOP model (minimal API or controller-based) hasn't changed
- `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web` in .NET 10 still works the same way

### 7. Build and Runtime Contract
- The `.sln` defines Debug/Release AnyCPU configurations
- `launchSettings.json` defines:
  - `TestProject` profile (Project): URLs `https://localhost:7146;http://localhost:5120`, development environment
  - `IIS Express` profile: `http://localhost:27057;https://localhost:44339`
- The `.gitignore` handles build outputs, user files, etc.

### Summary of Findings

1. **Target Framework**: Must change from `net8.0` to `net10.0` in the .csproj
2. **Static Files**: Currently serving `wwwroot` via `UseStaticFiles()`. For SPA routing, need `UseDefaultFiles()` + fallback or equivalent
3. **Home Directory Config**: Not present in any appsettings. Must be added. Options pattern via `IOptions<T>` is the standard ASP.NET Core approach. Could use `appsettings.json` or environment variable
4. **Controller/API Structure**: Single placeholder `TestController` with `[Route("[controller]")]`. Need a new controller for file operations with explicit routes like `api/files/browse`, `api/files/search`, etc.
5. **Middleware Pipeline**: Bare minimal. Need to ensure proper ordering for SPA + API coexistence (static files → API → SPA fallback)
6. **No NuGet dependencies**: Implicit ASP.NET Core packages only. No need for additional packages since everything needed (File I/O, JSON, Controllers) is built-in
7. **Solution file**: Standard VS 2022 format, fine for .NET 10
8. **Launch profiles**: Ports and launch URL set in `launchSettings.json`. The `launchUrl` of `index.html` suggests the SPA is served from root
9. **CSRF / Security**: `[ApiController]` provides automatic antiforgery validation? Actually it does NOT. But file operations should consider validation
10. **IIS Express profile**: Has `launchUrl: "weatherforecast"` which is the old default template - this is a leftover and should be updated to `index.html` or removed

content.text:
The project builds cleanly under net10.0 with just a TFM change — no compatibility issues. Now let me compile my full findings.

---

## Scouting Report: C# Backend Web API & .NET Project Configuration

### Investigation completed. Here is my full report.

---

## 1. Project Build Configuration

### csproj (`TestProject.csproj`)
- **SDK**: `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web` — correct for ASP.NET Core Web API
- **Target Framework**: Currently `net8.0` → **Must change to `net10.0`**
- **Nullable**: `enable` — good, keep
- **ImplicitUsings**: `enable` — good, keeps `using` boilerplate minimal
- **NuGet packages**: None. Zero explicit `<PackageReference>` entries. The implicit ASP.NET Core shared framework provides everything needed: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc`, file I/O (`System.IO`), JSON serialization (`System.Text.Json`), logging, etc.
- **ItemGroup**: Only includes `wwwroot\index.html` as a `<None>` item. This is fine but may need to be expanded to include the full wwwroot tree (e.g., `**/*`) if more static assets are added.
- **No `<OutputType>`** — implicit `Exe` from `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web`

**Relevance to task**: The TFM must be updated to `net10.0`. No extra NuGet packages are required (System.IO + System.Text.Json are in-box). The `<None Include="wwwroot\index.html" />` should be widened to `wwwroot\**\*` or simply removed (the SDK automatically includes wwwroot content for Web SDK projects).

### Solution file (`TestProject.sln`)
- Format: VS 2022 `Format Version 12.00`, `VisualStudioVersion = 17.7.34003.232`
- Single project: `TestProject.csproj` with GUID `{A08F391E-2070-4DE8-87D9-CED3E0A05AE9}`
- Debug and Release configurations for Any CPU
- Project type GUID: `{FAE04EC0-301F-11D3-BF4B-00C04F79EFBC}` (standard C# project)

**Relevance to task**: No changes needed. The solution format is compatible with .NET 10 SDK / VS 2022. The GUIDs identify the project type — they are fine as-is.

---

## 2. Middleware Pipeline (`Program.cs`)

Current pipeline (in order):
1. `builder.Services.AddControllers()` — registers MVC controller services
2. `app.UseHttpsRedirection()` — HTTP→HTTPS redirect
3. `app.UseStaticFiles()` — serves static files from `wwwroot/`
4. `app.MapControllers()` — maps attribute-routed controllers
5. `app.Run()` — starts the server

**Issues and observations:**
- **No `UseDefaultFiles()`**: The `launchUrl` is `index.html`, but there's no `UseDefaultFiles()` middleware. Static files work because of explicit URL, but `app.UseDefaultFiles()` before `UseStaticFiles()` would allow `https://localhost:7146/` to serve `index.html` directly.
- **No SPA fallback**: For deep-linkable SPA URLs (task requirement), if a user navigates to `https://localhost:7146/some/path` directly, it would 404. A fallback to `index.html` is needed. Options:
  - `app.MapFallbackToFile("index.html")` — built-in, available in .NET 8+
  - Custom middleware
- **No CORS middleware**: Not required if API and SPA are served from same origin (same port), which they are.
- **No `UseAuthorization()`**: The .NET 10 controller template adds this; it's harmless but unnecessary if no auth is implemented. The task doesn't mention authentication.
- **No OpenAPI**: Our project doesn't include OpenAPI; the .NET 10 default template adds it. The task doesn't require it. Fine to omit.
- **Pipeline ordering**: For the SPA + API combo, the typical order should be: HTTPS redirection → Static files → Controllers → SPA fallback. This ensures API routes are tried before the catch-all SPA fallback.

**Relevance to task**: The pipeline needs `UseDefaultFiles()` + `MapFallbackToFile("index.html")` for proper SPA deep-linking. The middleware order must ensure API routes take precedence over the SPA fallback. Current setup will work for basic file serving but will break deep links.

---

## 3. Routing & Controller Conventions (`Controllers/TestController.cs`)

- **Attribute**: `[ApiController]` — provides automatic model validation, problem details, attribute routing
- **Route**: `[Route("[controller]")]` — uses the convention token `[controller]` → resolves to `Test` (controller class name minus "Controller" suffix)
- **DI**: `ILogger<TestController>` injected via constructor
- **Action**: Single `[HttpGet]` returning `string`

**Current routes:**
- `GET /Test` → returns `"API Response"`

**What the task needs:**
- A new controller (e.g., `FilesController` or `ApiController`) with these endpoints:
  - `GET /api/browse?path=...` — list directory contents (JSON)
  - `GET /api/search?query=...&path=...` — search files/folders (JSON)
  - `POST /api/upload` — upload a file (multipart form-data)
  - `GET /api/download?path=...` — download a file
  - Bonus: `DELETE /api/delete?path=...` — delete file/folder
  - Bonus: `POST /api/move` — move/rename
  - Bonus: `POST /api/copy` — copy

**Route design considerations:**
- Using `/api/...` prefix clearly separates API routes from SPA routes
- `[Route("api/[controller]")]` on the controller, or explicit `[Route("api/browse")]` etc.
- Could also use a single controller with `[Route("api")]` and individual `[HttpGet("browse")]` etc.
- The `[ApiController]` attribute should be kept for consistent error handling

**Relevance to task**: The `TestController` is a placeholder. A new file operations controller must be added. The routing convention should use `/api/...` prefix to avoid conflicts with SPA routes.

---

## 4. Configurable Home Directory

**Current state**: No home directory configuration exists anywhere.

**Where to add it:**

The task says "A server side home directory should be configurable via variable" — this could mean an environment variable or a config file setting.

**Best approach (standard ASP.NET Core pattern):**

Create a POCO options class:
```csharp
public class FileServiceOptions
{
    public string HomeDirectory { get; set; } = "."; // default to current directory
}
```

Register in `Program.cs`:
```csharp
builder.Services.Configure<FileServiceOptions>(
    builder.Configuration.GetSection("FileService"));
```

In `appsettings.json`:
```json
{
  "FileService": {
    "HomeDirectory": "/home/blake/files"
  }
}
```

Or via environment variable: `FileService__HomeDirectory` (double underscore is the standard .NET config key separator for hierarchical keys).

Or simpler: just read from `IConfiguration` directly in the service/controller.

**Why the Options pattern**: It's the idiomatic approach, supports automatic reload, works with all configuration sources (appsettings, env vars, CLI args, user secrets), and is testable.

**Relevance to task**: A `FileServiceOptions` class with `HomeDirectory` must be defined and bound from configuration. The `appsettings.json` file is the natural place for the default value, with environment variables overriding it in production.

---

## 5. Launch Settings (`Properties/launchSettings.json`)

- **TestProject profile** (the main one):
  - `commandName: "Project"`
  - `launchBrowser: true`, `launchUrl: "index.html"` — starts browser at the SPA
  - `applicationUrl: "https://localhost:7146;http://localhost:5120"` — both HTTP and HTTPS
  - `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT: "Development"`

- **IIS Express profile**:
  - `launchUrl: "weatherforecast"` — **this is a leftover from the old default template** and should be updated to `index.html` or removed

**Relevance to task**: The `launchUrl: "index.html"` in the main profile confirms the SPA is the entry point. The IIS Express profile's stale `weatherforecast` URL is a minor cleanup item. No other changes needed unless port conflicts arise.

---

## 6. Configuration Files (`appsettings.json`, `appsettings.Development.json`)

- **`appsettings.json`**: Contains `Logging` and `AllowedHosts` only. No `FileService` section yet.
- **`appsettings.Development.json`**: Contains `Logging` overrides only.
- **`.gitignore`** already ignores `appsettings.*.local.json` and `secrets.json`, so local overrides are safe.

**Relevance to task**: Both files will need a `"FileService": { "HomeDirectory": "..." }` section added. The development version can point to a test directory; the release version will point to the actual server directory.

---

## 7. Static Files & wwwroot

- `wwwroot/index.html` is the SPA entry (currently "Hello World!" placeholder)
- `.gitignore` has patterns for `wwwroot/dist/` and `wwwroot/lib/`, suggesting anticipation of client-side build output
- The csproj explicitly includes `wwwroot/index.html` but this is unnecessary — `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web` automatically publishes `wwwroot/**/*`

**Relevance to task**: The SPA client-side code (TypeScript/JS, CSS, HTML) will live in `wwwroot/`. The `UseStaticFiles()` middleware in `Program.cs` will serve it. If a build step is used (TypeScript compilation), output should go to `wwwroot/dist/` which is already gitignored. The SPA needs to be deep-linkable — see middleware section above.

---

## 8. Key Compatibility Findings

- **net8.0 → net10.0 TFM change** builds successfully with zero code changes (verified)
- `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web` is available in .NET 10 with identical semantics
- `AddControllers()` / `MapControllers()` pattern unchanged
- `System.IO`, `System.Text.Json`, `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc` all in-box
- No NuGet package updates needed for the core ASP.NET stack
- The `.sln` file format (VS 2022) is fully compatible with .NET 10 SDK

---

## 9. Items Requiring Changes Summary

| Area | Current State | Required Change |
|---|---|---|
| `.csproj` TFM | `net8.0` | → `net10.0` |
| `.csproj` wwwroot include | `<None Include="wwwroot\index.html" />` | Remove or widen to `wwwroot\**\*` |
| `Program.cs` - middleware | Missing SPA fallback | Add `UseDefaultFiles()` + `MapFallbackToFile("index.html")` |
| `Program.cs` - order | Controllers after static files | Keep order: StaticFiles → Controllers → SPA fallback |
| `Controllers/TestController.cs` | Placeholder `Get()` returning string | Replace with proper file operations controller (or add new one) |
| `appsettings.json` | No `FileService` section | Add `"FileService": { "HomeDirectory": "..." }` |
| `appsettings.Development.json` | No `FileService` section | Add development home directory override |
| `launchSettings.json` - IIS Express | `launchUrl: "weatherforecast"` | Update to `"index.html"` |
| New: Options class | Doesn't exist | Create `FileServiceOptions` with `HomeDirectory` property |
| New: File operations service | Doesn't exist | Create service with Browse, Search, Upload, Download, Delete, Move, Copy methods |

---

## 10. Data Flow Summary (What the Pipeline Should Look Like)

```
HTTP Request
│
├─▶ UseHttpsRedirection()          ← redirect HTTP→HTTPS
│
├─▶ UseDefaultFiles()              ← map "/" to "index.html"
│
├─▶ UseStaticFiles()               ← serve wwwroot assets
│
├─▶ MapControllers() /api/browse    ← API returns JSON
│                    /api/search
│                    /api/upload
│                    /api/download
│                    /api/delete
│                    /api/move
│                    /api/copy
│
└─▶ MapFallbackToFile("index.html") ← SPA deep-link fallback
                                       (must be last)
```

The file service controller reads the configured `HomeDirectory` from `IOptions<FileServiceOptions>`, and all file operations are rooted there (i.e., the API only allows access to paths within the home directory to prevent path traversal).

---

This covers everything relevant to the C# backend Web API & .NET project configuration for the task. The changes are minimal: TFM bump, middleware additions for SPA support, home directory configuration, and a new file operations controller + service.
